data.title: Thermo Fisher Scientific: More Upside Likely As Headwinds Continue To Fade And Valuation Still Attractive
data.description:
Investment Thesis

I last covered Thermo Fisher Scientific Inc. (NYSE:TMO)(NEOE:TMO:CA) in August with a buy rating, and the stock has performed nicely since then, gaining ~25% versus S&P 500’s (SPY) 6.4% gains. Moving forward, the stock has good revenue and margin growth prospects. The company’s revenues should benefit from fading uncertainty around drug pricing and tariffs, which impact its core pharma customers. In addition, reshoring of pharma manufacturing, recovering clinical research business, easing comps, and continued M&As should help revenues.

Margins should benefit from operating leverage, continuous improvement under the PPI business system, which now includes open AI integration, and synergy savings as the company integrates recent acquisitions. The stock is trading at a slight discount versus historical levels despite recovering trends, and I believe there is still a good upside left. Hence, I continue to rate the stock a buy.

Revenue Analysis and Outlook

Thermo Fisher posted solid numbers in Q3 2025, with total revenue up about 5% year over year. Organic growth came in at 3%, M&A added roughly 1%, and foreign exchange contributed about 1%.

Looking at the segments, Life Sciences Solutions led the way with 8% revenue growth, including 5% organic, helped by strong performance in the bioproduction business and the research and safety market channel. Analytical Instruments was up 5%, with 4% organic growth, mainly driven by strength in electron microscopy and chromatography/mass spectrometry. Specialty Diagnostics delivered 4% growth, including 2% organic, supported by solid results in transplant diagnostics and immuno-diagnostics. The Laboratory Products and Biopharma Services segment grew 4% as well, with 3% organic, though that included about a 1% drag from the runoff of pandemic-related revenues.

Looking ahead, I am optimistic about Thermo Fisher's revenue growth prospects. Some of the headwinds that weighed on the company's growth over the last few quarters are starting to ease.

One of the major overhangs for big pharma companies, which are the major customers for Thermo Fisher, has been uncertainty around the U.S. drug pricing, as the Trump Administration called for significantly lower prices. This resulted in worries around the R&D budget of these pharma companies, which directly impacts TMO's sales. Tariffs also further complicated this situation.

However, there have been some signs of improvement of late. Earlier this month, Pfizer (PFE) signed a deal with the US government to sell drugs at lower prices through the TrumpRx portal and invest in the U.S. production in exchange for a reprieve in tariffs. This has improved the sentiments, and investors are expecting more such deals for other pharma companies, which should remove near-term uncertainty for the sector. This bodes well for Thermo Fisher's business.

The company is also poised to benefit as pharma and biotech customers expand their capacity in the U.S., aligning with the broader reshoring mandate. This should create incremental demand beyond day-to-day operations, especially for the company's bioproduction equipment and analytical instruments, and serve as a multi-year tailwind for the company's sales.

The company's Clinical Research business, which accounts for approximately one-fifth of its total revenue, is also poised to recover. Management pointed out that authorizations are up nicely and running well ahead of the current revenue levels. The sales in this business are also recovering and went from a mid-single-digit Y/Y decline in Q1 to flat in Q2 and up low single digits in Q3. I expect this acceleration to continue given strong authorization trends.

The company's other businesses, where the trends have been slow, are stabilizing as well. Academic and Government sales were down slightly Y/Y in Q3, but improved sequentially from Q2 as the funding visibility got a bit better compared to the uncertain environment earlier this year when the Trump Administration just started announcing the budget cuts related to universities.

China was soft, but trends are not getting incrementally worse, and given easier comparisons next year, it can move closer to flattish revenue performance next year. The runoff of pandemic-related revenue was another headwind and shaved one percentage point off 2025 growth, but this headwind will not repeat in 2026, helping growth acceleration.

Management has guided for a 3-6% organic growth target for the next couple of years. We are already at the low end of this range. Given the accelerating trends, I believe the company can easily achieve the mid to high end of this range in the coming years.

In addition to organic growth, the company should also benefit from bolt-on M&As. The recently acquired Solventum’s Filtration and Separation business (~$750 mn in annual revenue) expands Thermo Fisher's bioprocessing capabilities, while the purchase of Sanofi’s sterile fill-finish site in New Jersey strengthens its U.S. pharma services footprint. Even after these acquisitions, net leverage sits at ~2.9x EBITDA, which is reasonable and gives flexibility for more deals. So, I expect bolt-on M&As to continue complementing organic growth.

Margin Analysis and Outlook

Thermo Fisher posted strong margin gains in Q3 2025, with adjusted operating margin up 100 basis points year over year to 23.3%. The improvement was mainly driven by the company’s PPI (Practical Process Improvement) business system and tight cost controls, which delivered solid productivity gains that more than offset tariff pressures, FX headwinds, and some unfavorable mix. Adjusted gross margin came in at 41.9%, up 10 basis points from last year, while adjusted SG&A expenses as a percentage of revenue improved to 15.5%.

By segment, Life Sciences Solutions’ adjusted operating margin hit 37.4%, up 200 basis points. Specialty Diagnostics followed with a 150-basis-point gain to 27.4%, and Laboratory Products and Biopharma Services expanded 100 basis points to 14.5%. The only laggard was Analytical Instruments, where margins were down 230 basis points to 22.6%, mostly due to tariff and FX impacts. That said, it still marked a sequential improvement from Q2, which is encouraging.

Moving forward, I am optimistic about the company's margin growth prospects. With the revenue growth outlook positive, I believe the company should benefit from operating leverage due to increased sales.

The company is also doing a good job in terms of cost discipline and productivity improvements. The company's Practical Process Improvement (PPI) business system, which embeds a continuous improvement mindset, is helping the company. The company has also integrated OpenAI's enterprise tools into key operational areas like product development, service delivery, and customer engagement. This should further help improve execution, cycle times, and extract value from the company's deep data sets.

In addition, integration of the recent acquisitions (Solventum, Sanofi’s sterile fill-finish site) should also help margins as the Company starts realizing revenue synergies.

Management has guided for a 50-70 bps operating margin improvement in FY26 and FY27. Given the company already posted a 100 bps Y/Y improvement in margin last quarter, and conditions are improving from next year with revenue growth accelerating and the company comping tariff headwinds, I believe it is achievable.

Valuation and Rating

Thermo Fisher is trading at 25.19x FY25 consensus EPS estimates of $22.71 and 23.23x FY26 consensus EPS estimates of $24.62. Over the past five years, the stock has traded at an average forward P/E of ~23.9x. Since we are already in October, it makes sense to look at valuation on FY26 estimates, and on that basis, the stock is trading at a discount to historical levels.

I don't think the valuation discount relative to historical levels is justified. After several slow years, we are now seeing the early signs of re-acceleration in both revenue and profit growth. Given the potential for growth acceleration starting next year, I believe the stock should trade at a premium.

Thermo Fisher has a strong track record of execution both organically and through M&A, with a defensive revenue mix given its healthcare exposure and recurring revenue base (~83% revenue is recurring).

I believe it can easily re-rate to mid-to-high 20s as investors become comfortable with accelerating revenue growth prospects. This re-rating, combined with high single/low double-digit EPS growth potential, leaves good upside for the stock over the coming years. Hence, I continue to maintain my buy rating on the stock.

Risks

In addition to organic growth, Thermo Fisher pursues bolt-on mergers and acquisitions (M&A) to drive growth. Inorganic growth is relatively riskier compared to organic growth, and there are risks related to overpaying for an acquisition, integration challenges, and failure to realize expected synergies. Any of these outcomes could negatively impact the stock performance.

Thermo Fisher’s recent share price strength has also been partly driven by improved sentiment following Pfizer’s deal with the U.S. government under the TrumpRx initiative. Investors are anticipating that similar agreements may follow for other pharmaceutical companies, which would be beneficial for Thermo Fisher’s business. However, if such deals fail to materialize or if the momentum behind this trend slows, it could dampen customer sentiment and weigh on the stock’s valuation.

data.title: Zoomd Technologies: How A Microcap AdTech Player Is Winning The Open Internet Battle
data.description:
Investment Thesis

Zoomd Technologies Ltd. (OTCPK:ZMDTF, TSXV:ZOMD:CA) is a small yet highly profitable advertising technology firm that is currently in hypergrowth. Despite this, the firm trades at extremely attractive valuations, both on a P/E and FCF growth basis, with the valuations being supported by strong execution of management and sector tailwinds.

Since executing a strategic repositioning in Q2 of 2023, the firm has been able to see growth as high as the high double digits Y/Y, and has turned from unprofitability to net income margins as high as 31%. As repositioning efforts commenced, the company has diversified further between clients and sectors, continued to expand into new, high-margin sectors, and reduced operating expenses through the use of AI and machine learning. On top of this, the firm currently holds no GAAP-accountable debt, strengthening the risk/reward of this investment.

These factors, combined with recent catalysts of bullish analyst coverage and partnerships that expand the reach of Zoomd, all strengthen my bullish thesis in the company.

As part of my analysis of Zoomd Technologies Ltd., I would like to initiate coverage with a "Strong Buy" rating.

Business Overview

Zoomd Technologies is a global digital advertising company specializing in the acquisition of mobile users through marketing on the open internet. The company fundamentally differentiates itself by making its performance the critical metric behind its business: the company only gets paid when clients acquire paying customers, instead of just clicks or impressions.

The company operates in what is called the "open internet", as opposed to the typical "walled garden" approach seen by companies such as Google, Meta, and TikTok.

Major platforms operating within the "walled garden" are effective in acquiring customers not only due to the massive amount of reach available but also due to the immense amount of user data these companies can use to better target their ads.

However, this comes at the expense of several factors. Advertisers often struggle with limited transparency and control, and increased crowding has led to costs significantly increasing, forcing advertisers to diversify into options outside these "walled gardens".

To be able to capture this huge chunk of advertiser budget split, Zoomd uses AI-driven optimizations as well as machine learning to understand how advertising can be best suited for each product and each geography, and displays the real-time information to the client through a single, unified dashboard.

Explosive Top And Bottom Line Growth

Zoomd has experienced tremendous growth in its top line. When observing the quarterly revenue numbers from the company's investor slide deck, as shown above, the company has shown 40% growth Y/Y, or 86% when looking at the TTM revenue compared to TTM revenue 1 year ago.

This explosive growth can be attributed to several factors. However, the most important one was due to the repositioning efforts announced by the firm in Q2 '23. The repositioning efforts aimed to diversify their clientele away from single industries, and into more sectors and geographies. Additionally, the firm massively slashed operating costs through headcount adjustments and the discontinuation of several low-potential business lines, such as their operation in the crypto space, which was shown to be a low-margin side of the business.

As previously reported, the cost reductions, which extend beyond headcount adjustments, are projected to result in annual savings of approximately $4 million. In addition to these cost savings, we have announced the discontinuation of several non-profitable and low-potential business lines. This strategic decision allows us to reallocate efforts and resources towards Zoomd’s core business. - Amit Bohemsky, Chairman of Zoomd

The repositioning efforts of the at-the-time newly appointed CEO, Ido Almany, who was formerly the CEO of American Express’ licensee in Israel, were a grand success, transitioning the firm from struggling to stay afloat, to one that maintains high, growing margins and carries strong fundamental trends.

These efforts are further observed through the operating costs as a percentage of revenue. The firm consistently has shown significant improvements in operational costs, driven by the implementation of AI and machine learning and headcount cuts, driving operational costs down to 16% from a high of 48% in Q1 '23. Such a strong improvement signals strong operating leverage in the business, driving net income margin to as high as 31% as of the last quarter, from the lows of deep unprofitability.

Extreme Undervaluation

Despite such explosive growth, strong positive rhetoric from management, and a clean balance sheet (no GAAP-accountable debt obligations, and 7.7M of cash net of all obligations), mentions of buyback programs and even M&A, the company still trades at ~10x P/E on a TTM basis, as seen on Seeking Alpha as of October 22nd.

Reverse DCF Model

From time to time, I like to include a reverse DCF model in my analysis. Personally, I do not put much faith into the projections of future revenues, incomes, and FCF unless the figures come from management themselves. In the case of Zoomd, while we have solid rhetoric from management, no formal guidance was disclosed. Therefore, I do not want to speculate on a potential growth rate for such a company. However, I can run a Reverse Discounted Cash Flows (RDCF) model to determine what kind of FCF growth the market is currently pricing in.

Some analysis assumptions:

  • WACC of 12.28% (based on a high beta and no cost of debt, due to 0 GAAP accountable obligations)
  • Perpetual Growth Rate: 3% (After 9 years of cash flows, highly conservative assumption)

There are many uncertainties in such an analysis, and it should not hold any ground as an investment thesis on its own. However, it does provide some color as a very high level analysis around what the market is currently pricing into this stock.

As we can see, an average FCF growth of ~20% over the next 9 years is incredibly minuscule, considering how quickly the company has grown in the last year, with over 200% FCF growth. This is further amplified by the fact that the company is functioning in a rapidly growing market, as highlighted by Zoomd themselves.

Bullish Analyst Coverage

Zoomd has very recently (October 22nd, 2025) received its first-ever analyst rating. The rating, from ATB Capital Markets, put an "Outperform" rating on the stock, with a price target of C$4.00 (currently ~65% upside, as of close on Oct. 22nd), highlighting the firm's scalability, profitable growth, and potential for valuation multiple expansion.

In-app consumer spending is ~$200bn annually, and acquiring new users is the lifeblood of these businesses. New users for entertainment, e-commerce, and online gambling are high-value. These clients are willing to pay attractive rates per user.

Clients pay for new user acquisition and other high-value KPIs. This results in high ROI, alongside advertising/marketing performance that is directly tied to client revenue and has identifiable return.

Favorable Catalysts

Continuing with the first point of the ATM Capital Markets analyst, new users for entertainment, e-commerce, and online gambling are considered high-value. This statement from the analyst could not have been timed better, as the firm has recently announced, through a press release, a strategic partnership with E2. E2-Quadrat is a global leader in digital marketing and technology solutions for the sports and betting industry.

This strategic partnership shows management's focus on continuing to expand their reach, specifically into sectors where advertising rates may command a premium.

This partnership reflects Zoomd’s strategy of aligning with category leaders in high-growth verticals. This recent expansion into new segments and partnerships reflects the company’s ongoing efforts to diversify its customer concentration - Amit Bohemsky, Chairman of Zoomd

Risks

Concentration Risk

While I believe the fundamentals of the business remain strong, as with any investment, there are always risks present. One risk that is often brought up with Zoomd is around customer concentration, specifically around their largest customer, which is speculated to be Shein.

While Zoomd has historically had significant issues with concentration, specifically in the realm of crypto, it now represents less than 10% of their clientele size.

However, the risk of concentration still persists within their portfolio, this time due to only 5 clients making up the majority of the revenue. During the Q1 '25 earnings call, investors were able to get the following information:

As we reported, 85% of our revenues come from 5 largest customers.

While this definitely will raise alarm bells for investors, it is also important to realize that each of these customers operates within different countries and even different continents, each with different KPIs and targets for each region. As reported by the CEO on the same earnings call, one of the top clients (speculated to be Shein) operates within more than 30 different countries. This signals continued trust in Zoomd to deliver on their KPIs, and that Shein has, over time, expanded its partnership with the firm due to steady and successful results.

This is further reinforced by the strong client retention rates that Zoomd has shown.

Business Model Erosion to Competition

Another concern mentioned by Zoomd investors centers around the rapid growth seen within the industry, specifically with peers such as APP (AppLovin).

In the case of AppLovin, the company operates in a fundamentally different way than Zoomd. First, AppLovin does not function within the open internet space in the same way that Zoomd does. AppLovin uses an AI-only approach to create ads only within the mobile apps using AppLovin's proprietary software. This is a stark difference from Zoomd, whose ads may appear in places spanning between websites, social platforms, or even influencers. In this sense, Zoomd can be considered a more traditional open internet operator.

Additionally, it is worth noting that due to the much broader potential reach of open internet advertising, as opposed to the less wide-spanning approach that APP has taken, Zoomd is better suited to tackle the wide variability within the fragmented open internet, which may require an approach that requires human intervention and industry knowledge/expertise, such as in the case of influencer campaigns.

Thus, while erosion of the business model is a possibility, at least in the near future, the two companies are not direct competitors, and it may take years before both companies mature to a point where they are competing for market share on a scale that directly impacts their top line.

Conclusion

Zoomd Technologies presents itself as a highly attractive micro-cap pick. The company has shown strong growth in not only the top line, but has also shown immense margin expansion, led by effective cost-cutting initiatives. Even with such growth, the firm trades at only 10x TTM P/E.

Despite being a micro-cap, which is typically associated with high risk, the firm has a clean balance sheet, and a strong management team that has pulled the company out of a dire situation, and has turned the situation into not only profitability, but even hyper-growth.

All this information, topped with strong sentiment from the recent analyst coverage, as well as management mentions of potential for stock buybacks and M&A activities, signals that the company has a strong footing over their core business and plans to reward shareholders with the fruits of their business.

I am initiating a "Strong Buy" rating on ZOMD:CA (or ZMDTF on the OTC), due to strong fundamentals and market demand, execution strength of management, and deep mispricing by the market.

Editor's Note: This article discusses one or more securities that do not trade on a major U.S. exchange. Please be aware of the risks associated with these stocks.

data.title: Strategy: Finally Time To Buy Again (Technical Analysis)
data.description:
Thesis Summary

MicroStrategy (NASDAQ:MSTR) has been sliding down for the last 3 months.

But after the recent crypto flush out, and given the nice bounce from the recent lows, I believe there’s a good setup for MSTR and potentially Bitcoin (BTC-USD) going forward.

MSTR’s dip looks buyable, both in terms of technicals and fundamentals.

A Quick Recap

My last MSTR post was almost six months ago.

Back then, I made the case that MSTR was reaching a buyable breakout. While the stock did go on to rally over the next month or so, we have been in a steady slide since mid-July.

More recently, I spoke about Bitcoin looking toppy, but with room for a final rally into the $150,00 zone.

Following the latest flush out in crypto, which saw $20 billion in capital wiped out, I believe we now have the right set-up in Bitcoin and Strategy to see a meaningful bounce, perhaps even new all-time highs.

The Fundamentals

The fundamentals still look supportive of higher Bitcoin prices. The “debasement trade”, which I covered recently in this post, is very much still in play.

The current administration wants to outgrow the deficit and do so with the help of a dovish Fed.

With inflation data coming out today weaker than expected, the road may now be clear for more cuts, and Powell has even talked about ending QT in the near future.

Plus, as I highlighted in my last gold article, the spectacular rally we have seen in gold could soon be followed by a similar rally in Bitcoin, if history is to repeat itself.

All this at a time when Crypto sentiment is still quite bearish. This is when you buy, not when you sell.

All in all, there’s a good confluence of macro tailwinds, bearish sentiment, and positioning that tells me we could be in for another fast and furious rally.

The Technicals

From a technical perspective, this seems like a good risk/reward setup.

Let’s start by looking at the underlying asset, Bitcoin.

For the time being, Bitcoin has bounced beautifully off the 200-day EMA, for now, and this could set the low.

Ideally, I would love to see a quick double bottom form, much like we saw back in March, with a quick dip below the 200 EMA, finding support at $97K and potentially forming a larger bullish divergence with the RSI.

Now, let’s look at MSTR.

Like Bitcoin, MSTR looks like it could be close to a bottom. The RSI has trended higher over the last few weeks, though arguably we are still below the key EMAs, so the chart looks a bit worse.

Zooming out to the weekly, we can see that MSTR is within an accumulation range that has been going on for months.

We saw this happen back in March 2024 before the big rally, and this could be a repeat.

Like Bitcoin, there’s room for a slight retest of lower lows, but either way, this price today seems quite good if we are indeed expecting a significant rally.

A simple retest of recent highs would imply a 60% rally from today’s price, which is nothing to scoff at.

MSTR over Bitcoin?

I would defend owning both, but I would expect MSTR to outperform Bitcoin if it rallies to new highs.

The current NAV premium for MSTR is historically low, close to 1.2, and I’d expect this to increase as Bitcoin rallies.

Why?

Firstly, because MSTR already has Bitcoin in its balance sheet, this increases its leverage potential.

The whole strategy behind Strategy works when Bitcoin is rising, and that is a reason there’s a premium.

On the other hand, FOMO and sentiment should also make retail names in crypto catch a bid.

With short interest near 10%, this also gives MSTR some short squeeze potential.

Risks

There’s also the chance that the Bitcoin cycle has ended, and we don’t actually see new highs for some time. This is a distinct possibility investors should not ignore.

We are nearing what would be the end of the Bitcoin 4-year cycle, and we are near important levels of support after the recent sell-off.

If Bitcoin breaks below $90K, this would be a big hit to my bull thesis.

Final Thoughts

All in all, I like the setup right now for MSTR and Bitcoin. This could be risky, of course, but there’s plenty of upside potential. If Bitcoin breaks towards new all-time highs, there’s room for MSTR to even double from here.

data.title: Gold Hits A Speedbump
data.description:
It has been a strange year in so many ways, but one of this year’s odder bedfellows has been the tandem duo of US stocks and gold. These two asset classes don’t normally tango together.

In 1979, gold was just about the only thing that was working for portfolios, while the S&P 500 was in the dumps and bond markets were roiled by the Volcker shock of ultra-tight monetary policy.

Two decades later, 1999 was a go-go year for any stock with even the flimsiest connection to the Internet, while gold had nothing to offer other than being pretty to look at.

This year, the precious metal and the blue-chip stock index have been at the forefront of a giddy general melt-up for financial assets. Gold, in particular, has been on a tear since late summer, shooting up by more than 30 percent from the middle of August to the end of last week.

But this week, suddenly, things went south and the price of gold dropped by nearly seven percent in just two days, no doubt to the chagrin of those coming late to the party.

To Hedge or Not to Hedge

The timing of this pullback was a little odd. As we wrote about in our column last week, all the talk of the town in recent days has been about a stock market bubble. Bubble talk, in general, gets people to start thinking about hedging.

Gold, of course, has long been thought of as a go-to hedge asset – witness that start turn during the stagflation miasma of the late 1970s. One might have thought, rationally, that this week would kick off with a big pullback in stocks while gold rose some more.

But rationality has precious little to do with the ways of the markets in these times of ours. Stocks have continued blithely to ignore anyone waving wildly at stratospheric valuation levels, surging to yet another record high by the end of the week.

It is entirely possible, of course, that gold’s performance this year doesn’t actually have all that much to do with hedging. There are other, potentially cheaper ways to offset exposure to US stocks if one is so inclined, including simple S&P 500 put options.

Capital markets are starting to look a lot more like prop bets than anything else, where you can take a position for or against just about any movement large or small in just about any corner of the globe using an ever-expanding arsenal of easy-to-obtain assets.

Maybe there’s nothing more to the post-August rally in gold than the simple fact of all that liquidity coursing around the world has to go somewhere, so a good chunk of it might as well go to something that has been around for thousands of years.

Just a Pretty Hunk of Meta

The other thing to remember about gold is that it has no other source of value than price appreciation. No interest coupons, no dividend payments, nothing other than a belief that the price will be higher tomorrow than it is today.

Yes, it is a scarce asset with a finite supply, and over long periods of time the scarcity factor delivers value. But not always. Gold rose, for understandable reasons, during and after the stock market crash in 2008, reaching a then-peak in 2011.

But prices fell after that, and didn’t regain that 2011 peak price until the middle of 2020. In the post-pandemic period through 2023 gold’s performance was mostly flat. It was no help to investors looking for a hedge during the equity bear market of 2022, with prices falling around 15 percent from April to October of that year (the sharp rise of interest rates during this period made gold, as a non-interest bearing asset, less attractive).

What happens next remains to be seen, of course. Today, stocks are up, gold is down and a belated inflation report (delayed due to the government shutdown) delivered no surprises versus expectations. In the wake of that powerful surge in gold prices from August to last week, though, a more protracted pullback would not be out of the question.

Original Post

Editor's Note: The summary bullets for this article were chosen by Seeking Alpha editors.

data.title: CEF Weekly Review: CLO Equity Continues To Struggle
data.description:
Welcome to another installment of our CEF Market Weekly Review, where we discuss closed-end fund [CEF] market activity from both the bottom-up – highlighting individual fund news and events – as well as the top-down – providing an overview of the broader market. We also try to provide some historical context as well as the relevant themes that look to be driving markets or that investors ought to be mindful of.

This update covers the period through the third week of October. Be sure to check out our other weekly updates covering the business development company [BDC] as well as the preferreds/baby bond markets for perspectives across the broader income space.

Market Action

It was an unusual week for the CEF market, as nearly all NAVs were higher and yet nearly all discounts were wider. Typically, discounts move in the same direction as NAVs (in price terms, i.e. discounts tend to tighten when NAVs move higher and vice-versa). This tells us that despite fairly strong markets, there is some anxiety on the part of CEF investors. We see a similar, if less pronounced, pattern on the month-to-date chart as well.

CEF sector discounts have widened back to their historic average level.

Market Themes

The CLO Equity CEF sector continues to struggle, with NAVs down again in September, with the average down around 3%. Least affected, as usual, are the partial-CLO equity funds like XFLT and EIC.

Year-to-date, NAVs are down close to 20%.

In Q3, the average fall was around 6%, which is quite a bit more than the NII generated by the funds. The story for NAV drops this year has been the tightening in credit spreads, and it’s true that spreads were tighter through Q3, though not by much. Anticipation of lower short-term rates, which accelerated in September on the back of Powell’s dovish pivot at the end of September, has been another driver.

Perhaps credit worries are affecting valuations as well. First Brands was not a big exposure in the overall CLO sector. On average it appears to be around 0.24% of total CLO assets. The implicit ~10x leverage means CLO Equity exposure could be north of 2%.

Market Commentary

The Pioneer loan CEF PHD announced its liquidating distribution of $9.738549. As was the case with HNW and PHT, this is quite a bit below the NAV of $10.12. The press release said once again that there might be additional distributions to come, which would make sense. Bank loans are not the most liquid assets, and the fund is likely shopping around for a better price on the rump portfolio. The price did not react much, which suggests the market expects to be made whole.

The FS Credit Opportunities Corp (FSCO) - a fund allocated in large part to private loans - was a big mover earlier in the week. There was nothing obvious here except, perhaps, a highlight of its First Brands exposure. This wasn’t exactly a secret. The fund has held a position for at least a year. Interestingly, it appears to have increased it from $34m to $54m most recently, which worked out to around 3.5% of the NAV. If fully written down, the loss would be a bit above its quarterly distribution - in effect, a loss of 4 months of income.

The fact that the fund held a position in what looks to be a house of cards looks bad not just in terms of the NAV loss but also in terms of what it implies about the rest of its portfolio. The fact that it increased its position shortly before the blow-up suggests an absence of the level of risk aversion evident in nearly all other public BDC/BDC-like entities. We continue to favor BDCs over CEFs with private loan exposure, such as FSCO.

data.title: TSMC And ASML: The Deep Value Engine Of The AI Revolution
data.description:
The value behind silicon

All technological revolutions have their own visible protagonists and invisible architects. The first dominated the headlines; the second consolidated the physical foundation that holds its history. In the current AI wave, I usually see a market that looks at companies that train models with fascination, design chips, or capture software rents, but rarely looks down, to the terrain where all this really becomes possible.

As Gartner’s data shows, 86% of companies plan to use AI to maintain or grow revenue, a signal that AI is no longer just a technological narrative but an economic force.

There, in those very foundations, are Taiwan Semiconductor Manufacturing Company (NYSE:TSM) and ASML Holding (ASML). Without TSMC, the mass production of advanced chips wouldn't exist, and without ASML, the technology that makes them possible wouldn't exist. They are two different pieces of a single cog, one represents the industrial scale, the other, the technological exclusivity.

Upon observing their metrics, I find an interesting paradox. While the market overpays the stories that are daily headlines, the profound value is still hidden in the infrastructure that makes them possible.

And between these two companies, the asymmetry is clear: TSMC trades with a discount, respecting its structural power, while ASML now reflects the perfection in its numbers. Over the past two years, ASML has traded around 28–30x EV/EBITDA, while TSMC remains near 17x, despite both delivering consistent growth and strong margins.

To me, this isn’t a reflection of fundamentals but of perception: the market rewards technological exclusivity (ASML) while undervaluing industrial execution and scale (TSM).

TSMC: Scale, efficiency, and an undervalued future

The Q3 2025 wasn't just a good quarter for TSMC; it was a validation of its model. The company reported net revenue of $33.1 B, a growth of 40.8% YoY, with a gross margin of 59.5% and an operative margin of 50.6%. They are numbers that few industrial companies manage to get a hold of, and that TSMC maintains with a consistency that seems to defy cycles.

The CFO, Wendell Huang, synthesized it with clarity:

Moving into fourth quarter 2025, we expect our business to be supported by continued strong demand for our leading-edge process technologies ”

When I read that, I don't see a conjunctural bounce. I see a structural demand for AI infrastructure and of high-performance computation (HPC), that has already become the new normal. Each breakthrough in language models, each expansion of data centres, needs denser, smaller, and more efficient chips. In that context, TSMC not only participates, but has also become indispensable.

The CEO, C.C. Wei added:

Our 2-nanometer and A16 technologies is addressing the insatiable demand for energy-efficient computing, and almost all innovators are working with TSMC. N2 is well on track for volume production later this quarter, with good yield. We expect a faster ramp in 2026, fueled by both smartphone and HPC AI applications"

I think of it as a key phrase, because it shows how TSMC now not only leads in technology, but also in industrial geopolitics. Its new plants in Japan (Kumamoto), the US (Arizona) and Germany (Dresden) are reconfiguring the global supply chain, reducing the dependence on Taiwan and strengthening its role as an essential provider for the Western World.

From a financial point of view, the numbers reinforce that story. With an EV/EBITDA FWD of 13.8x, a P/E FWD of 28.5x, and an ROE of 35%, TSMC trades as if it were a cyclical industry, when it actually is a structural compounder. Its FCF yield (~3%) and its net cash position allow it to expand without compromising profitability.

To me, the market still remains anchored to the geopolitical fear. But that relationship is double–edged, given that the market needs TSMC as TSMC needs stability. That interdependence is, to me, is highest security margin.

ASML and the price of exclusivity

And while TSMC represents the scale, ASML represents exclusivity. It's the only company capable of fabricating extreme ultraviolet lithography equipment (EUV), the tool without which no advanced chip could exist. Each GPU, each CPU, and each three-nanometer (or less) wafer has its signature written all over it.

In Q3 2025, ASML reported €7.5B in revenue and a net income of €2.1B, with a gross margin of 51.6% and an operative margin of 32.8%. The net booking reached €5.4B, of which €3.6B came from EUV equipment, confirming that the demand still remains solid in a more moderate environment.

Its CEO,

Christophe Fouquet, summed it up this way:

(...) we said that most probably AI will drive more advanced applications in semiconductors. So advanced DRAM, advanced Logic. This is happening and this is driving more advanced litho, higher litho intensity. We expect that to continue”

I agree with that vision. ASML is not only a provider, but the modern industrial world's most sophisticated bottleneck. The company projects that the semiconductors' global market will surpass $1T towards 2030, with potential income of between €44B and €60B and gross margins that could near 60%. In other words, it doesn't build equipment; it builds the frontiers of technological progress.

But even perfection has its price, given that when I analyze ASML's metrics, I observe that it trades at a P/E FWD of 35.7x and a EV/EBITDA FWD of 27.5x, with a 25% boost before TSMC. Its 53.9% ROE and its 29% FCF margin are spectacular, but the numbers' expansion margin is limited.

Moreover, its exposure to exportation regulations towards China and the dependence on its clients' investment cycle introduce risks that the market tends to minimize. In short terms, an unrepeatable company, but already valued as such.

Risks, of what the market does not always discount

The appeal of both companies doesn't erase the risks that surround them. In the case of TSMC, I consider the most evident one still to be the geopolitical one. The tension between China and Taiwan not only generates political uncertainty, but also makes an impact on the global perception of the risk. Even if the international expansion mitigates part of this scenario, an escalation could alter logistic chains or temporally affect the market's trustworthiness.

I also see a technological concentration risk, due to its dependence on clients like Nvidia (NVDA) , Apple (AAPL) or AMD (AMD) exposing it to eventual demand changes. A twist in the strategy of any of those players could reduce margin in the short term, even if it doesn't alter the structural value of the business.

In ASML, in the meantime, the main risk is regulatory. The US and the European Union's export controls towards China could limit its growth in one of the most relevant markets for its EUV systems. To that is added a highly specialized supply chain, that increases its vulnerability before technological or material disruptions.

Both share too the cycle risk, given that the semiconductor industry still remains intensive in capital and sensitive to the investment spending of its clients. However, in my experience, businesses with margins, technology, and scale like theirs happen to turn out reinforced on each correction.

The profound value, or two halves of the industrial brain

After presenting the results separately, I consider it necessary to think of both at the same time, given that that vision could allow us to understand how modern industrial power is built upon. ASML marks the beginning of the cycle with requests that anticipate the sector's expansion, and TSMC transforms that expectancy in production, margins, and cash flow. One defines the limits of physics; the other turns them into economy.

From my perspective, AI accelerates that symbiosis, given that each chip generation needs a new tool generation, and each ASML innovation is translated into a new wave of income for TSMC. They are, in essence, the two halves of a single industrial brain: one thinks and the other executes.

If I am left with something at all from this comparison, is the sensation that the market pays too much for perfection and too little for execution. ASML is the technological jewel; TSMC, the muscle that turns that technology into reality. Both are indispensable, but only one keeps trading below its true role in the system.

Conclusion: The visible and invisible

The market likes visible stories, but the most lasting value usually hides in what can't be seen. While the headlines talk about NVDA, OpenAI (OPENAI) or Microsoft (MSFT), the future material of that intelligence is written in the ASML labs and the TSMC fabrics. One designs the tool that records the light silicon; the other turns that silicon into electronical thought.

The way I see it, TSMC is a buy, because the ranking doesn't reflect a structural role nor its price fixation power; while for ASML, my position switches to hold, because its perception is now in price and its challenge is to hold on to it.

The profound value doesn't shine on the screens, nor is it measured by the headlines. Instead, it's in the invisible structures that sustain human intelligence. And on this cycle, those structures carry two names: TSMC and ASML.

data.title: WEEK: Weekly Payouts Are Highly Overrated
data.description:
In my last article published on IAUI, I had mentioned how I was looking at gold as a part of research to create high-yield, capital preservation portfolios suitable as collateral for loans. Naturally, the assets that offer some of the best loan-to-value ("LTV") ratios and margin requirements are short-term U.S. treasuries, which has led me to spend a significant amount of time looking at the various ways to invest in T-Bills. One such way is through the Roundhill Weekly T-Bill ETF (BATS:WEEK). This ETF differs from other T-Bill ETFs due to its weekly payouts, which could be potentially advantageous in reducing loan balances or compounding returns faster. But was this increased payout frequency worth higher management fees? The short answer is no. Short-term U.S. treasury ETFs with lower fees, such as the iShares® 0-3 Month Treasury Bond ETF (SGOV), will outpace WEEK even with monthly payouts. And while I wouldn't sell WEEK for SGOV due to marginally different results and the potential for WEEK to be more tax-efficient, I wouldn't buy WEEK over other alternatives until WEEK's tax considerations are confirmed. Thus, I rate WEEK a Hold.

WEEK's Underperformance

WEEK is an ETF that holds U.S. Treasury bills with maturities between 0 and 3 months, and its primary differentiating factor from other T-Bill ETFs is its weekly payout frequency. For this weekly payout, the fund has a 0.19% expense ratio, which is considerably higher than many alternatives. SGOV, for example, is one of the most popular short-term U.S. T-Bill ETFs, and its expense ratio is less than half of WEEK's at 0.09%.

This higher expense ratio has caused a considerable drag on WEEK's performance. Since the fund's inception, WEEK's total returns have lagged other ETFs by approximately 10 to 14 basis points.

While this may not seem like much, keep in mind that WEEK's inception was on March 6, 2025, and the performance gap is only expected to widen over the long term.

Can Weekly Payouts Justify Higher Expenses?

The thing about weekly payments, however, is that the increased payment frequency can potentially lead to faster compounding or loan repayments compared to monthly payouts. For example, let's take a look at a scenario where you have $100K in an asset that yields 5% and a $50K loan with an interest rate of 3%, and compare weekly versus monthly payouts. Over the span of five years, the asset that pays weekly will help you pay down the loan faster than the asset that pays monthly. The difference is about $90, where you'll have a $31,006.52 remaining loan balance on weekly payouts and $31,096.50 from monthly distributions.

This, however, assumes that both assets are only different in repayment frequencies and are otherwise equal. In the case of WEEK versus something like SGOV, this is very much not the case due to the higher expense ratios. If we take a similar study but use WEEK's and SGOV's actual distributions, we'll find that WEEK's weekly payouts have not done enough to offset its higher expense ratio. The following shows how 500 shares in WEEK would have done against SGOV when paying off a $50K loan at 3% between February 15 and October 22, 2025.

Of course, the problem with this study is that potentially more important than the payment frequency is when the payments are applied. Because I had included SGOV's distribution on March 6, WEEK's weekly payout benefits cannot be effectively identified, as the more frequent payments are not reducing the loan balance lower than the lump sum payment made initially. Indeed, if we do adjust the timing of this study to begin on March 12 and exclude SGOV's March 6 distribution, WEEK has lowered the loan balance faster.

When you look at this chart, though, you'll see that SGOV's payouts are outpacing WEEK's. The history of WEEK is too short to provide conclusive evidence from historical data, but if we extrapolate SGOV and WEEK's loan repayment rates, we'll see that SGOV is projected to eventually pay down the loan faster than WEEK.

Thus, WEEK's weekly payouts do not justify its higher expense ratio unless you attribute it to a unique, invaluable benefit that is difficult to quantify. For example, you could potentially get more value out of WEEK if its weekly payment allowed you to reinvest in some other asset at a better price because you got your distribution earlier than SGOV. This, however, depends heavily on the individual.

WEEK's Potential Tax Benefit

What could change everything, though, is WEEK's potential tax benefit over other ETFs. Per its 19a-1 notices, WEEK's distributions have often been estimated to include a significant portion as return of capital ("ROC"). Its last one on October 21, 2025, for example, lists approximately 53% of its distribution as ROC. This could be a significant tax advantage because ROC is a distribution that is not immediately taxable and instead lowers your investment cost-basis. Eventually, you will be taxed on distributions when your cost basis reaches zero or when you sell for a profit, but even then, these taxes will generally be considered capital gains rather than the ordinary income taxes that interest income is typically subject to though it is worth mentioning that SGOV's payouts are generally qualified interest income that are exempt from many U.S. government taxes. Nonetheless, if WEEK's distributions truly included large portions of ROC, then the fund would be greatly advantageous to other ETFs for those investing in taxable accounts. Of course, please consult a professional tax advisor for your specific situation.

But before you invest WEEK for its potential tax benefits, we must note that 19a-1 notices are only estimates. A fund does not simply categorize its distributions as ROC because it feels like it. It requires things like an actual return of your own principal or tax-loss harvesting strategies, where losses in other parts of the portfolio can offset gains and give an opportunity for an ETF to distribute some of its earnings as ROC. In the case of WEEK, it's hard to see how the fund would be able to do this. When we look at the fund's Semi-Annual Financial Statements for June 30, 2025, it should be noted that WEEK has not reported any ROC distributions.

So until further information is given that confirms WEEK's ability to pay ROC distributions, I would not buy WEEK in anticipation that the fund will be more tax-efficient compared to its peers.

WEEK's Verdict

Until then, I rate WEEK a Hold. For those that already hold WEEK, there's not much of a reason to sell and switch to alternative lower-cost ETFs given the marginal differences and potential for WEEK to be more tax-efficient. I, however, also wouldn't buy WEEK, thinking that it will be more tax-efficient. It's hard to imagine how much ROC WEEK can really produce, given the cash-like nature of 0 to 3-month U.S. T-Bills, and its weekly payouts have not been shown to actually offset its higher expense ratios. Thus, when it comes to buying short-term U.S. T-Bills via ETFs, low fees are still ultimately your most important consideration, and I would stick with something like SGOV unless WEEK lowers its fees enough or confirms tax-efficiency.

data.title: Webull: Strong Growth Story, But Valuation Demands Patience
data.description:
Webull Corporation (NASDAQ:BULL) is an American financial services company that focuses on digital investments and offers a trading platform to its customers. BULL emphasizes a seamless user experience and provides brokerage services for trading stocks, options, ETFs, and other instruments.

The company went public on April 11 via a merger with a SPAC. BULL experienced a massive demand for its stock, surging more than 300% in the first few trading days, but has been in a downward channel since then. The stock is down roughly 49% over the past 6 months, indicating that the sell-off may appear overdone. However, I argue that the stock remains expensive, and further consolidation is a possibility.

The stock currently trades at a forward P/E of around 103, highlighting a nearly 9.5x premium versus the sector median of about 11x. The stock may rebound from these levels, but I think the market will wait for management to execute. Therefore, I think Webull is a Hold with a $12 price target over the next 12 months. This represents roughly a 10% upside possibility, but market-like performance rather than outperformance.

Q2 2025: Strong Revenue Growth And High-Demand For Services

On August 28, the company reported Q2 2025 results. This was a strong quarter for the company, highlighting significant revenue growth and strong demand for its services, yet the market met the results with a roughly 7% sell-off over the next trading day.

Webull delivered $131.5 million in revenue, underscoring about a 46% increase on a year-over-year basis. As management highlighted in their presentation, this was driven by significant trading revenue growth, underlining 63% growth versus the same quarter last year, signaling higher volumes.

On the bottom line, the company delivered a net loss of $1.20 per ordinary share. I think this was a key reason that highly contributed to a post-earnings sell-off. On the other hand, Webull focuses heavily on its revenue growth and customer expansion. Therefore, I would not be surprised if the bottom line may remain disappointing over the next several quarters. Furthermore, bottom-line decline supports a bearish stance on the company and may suggest continued consolidation.

On the upside, the financial services company has achieved an all high in customer assets. The figure has reached $15.9 billion, indicating a 64% increase on a year-over-year basis. Management highlighted that registered users grew to 24.9 million, representing 18% growth over the past 12 months, and 4.73 million in terms of funded accounts, up 9% since the same quarter last year.

I think this was a significant quarter for the company, underlining a strong demand for its services. Nevertheless, management announced it has re-entered crypto offerings in the US, and launched crypto services in Brazil and Australia, which could heavily drive customer growth in the near-term future. To that, on September 8th, Webull announced it had entered European markets with the Dutch brokerage launch.

Given a significant focus on revenue and customer growth, I think that the trend of business expansion is likely to continue. However, until the bottom line stabilizes, the company may continue trading at rich valuations, supporting my neutral stance, highlighting that upside may be limited in the near term.

The company is estimated to announce its Q3 2025 results on November 12. Wall Street anticipates $137 million in revenue at the midpoint, and $0.03 in diluted EPS. This would indicate a continuation in the business growth, signaling strong demand for its services.

It would also come in line with the previous quarters' revenue estimates, but would result in a significant bottom-line surprise. I think that beat on the top-line is likely, but I am not sure whether the company can achieve the diluted EPS estimate.

Management has substantial focus on customer growth and business expansion internationally, which I believe may stress the bottom-line figure over the next years. Time will tell, but a turnaround on the diluted EPS figure may be a game-changer for this financial services company, yet I am not sure whether that is the core focus for the company, and whether we will see a bottom-line rebound from this quarter.

Webull Trades At A Premium, And It May Need Time To Rebound

Webull currently trades at a forward P/E of 103.14, which represents a massive premium versus both the sector median of 10.74 and the average company in the S&P 500 at a 23.10 earnings multiple.

It does not necessarily mean that the company cannot trade at this premium further, but I argue that investors may seek other alternatives in the market, that has a more significant market share and trade at a lower earnings multiple, such as Robinhood at a forward P/E of 63.40 and 26.7 million in funded customers. If that happens, this could substantially stress the stock, and downward momentum may continue.

The financial services company trades at a Price/Sales ratio of 10.11, indicating roughly a 3.5x premium versus peers of 2.84. This suggests that the market may have already priced in the future revenue growth. Therefore, even if management continues to execute well, this would not necessarily translate into stock price appreciation or multiple expansion.

On the upside, Webull appears to be a low-leverage business, and that meaningfully contributes to its bullish outlook. The company has about $113 million in total obligations. Regardless, the financial services business company has $1.67 billion in cash and could pay its debt with ease.

Given its capital structure, it appears that Webull is well-positioned to operate in both high and low interest-rate environments. For instance, last quarter the company generated $36.3 million in total interest and dividend income, which supports my thesis that high-yield accounts offer a compelling opportunity to benefit from a high-interest environment.

Webull has been a phenomenal top-line growth story, so far. The company grew revenue by 26.51% over the past 12 months, implying nearly 3.4x outperformance versus peers of 7.70%. This supports a bullish narrative for the company and partially justifies the brokerage firm trading at a premium.

BULL operates at an above-market gross profit margin (TTM) of 78%. This implies a significant outperformance versus the sector median of 60%, supporting an elevated multiple. The net income margin (TTM) of negative 3% balances the bullish outlook and presents a disadvantage versus peers of 23%. However, as I have mentioned, this is mainly due to the company's massive business expansion.

This article highlights that Gen Z starts investing early, with 54% of the survey respondents beginning by the age of 21. I think this underscores a massive demographic tailwind for the company. With more people investing and by doing so at an early age, such financial services disruptors as Webull may appear more favorable than traditional banks. These companies are offering an investing and trading platform at low costs, including a large variety of instruments, including crypto, which plays a significant role when choosing a services provider. This may help to establish a sticky customer base, which could lay the foundation for the company in the years ahead.

Wall Street anticipates $0.11 in diluted EPS for FY2025 and $0.17 for FY2026, implying about 62% bottom-line growth from an FY2025 estimate. I think that this is a reasonable estimate. The financial services provider has major tailwinds tied to demographics and early investors, which may appear to be more favorable for retail investors versus traditional banks, given its low-cost policy and all-in-one trading platform.

Webull aims to heavily expand, including other continents other than North America. If the company succeeds, it could significantly impact both top and bottom line growth over the following years. If we apply a 70x earnings multiple, which could be more than 30% below the current forward P/E, to an FY2026 diluted EPS estimate of $0.17, we come in at my 12-month price target of about $12.

This presents a continued consolidation, but an elevated multiple given double-digit revenue growth and international expansion. I think BULL is likely to present market-like returns, rather than significantly outperform the benchmark. This may appear to be a compelling opportunity for long-term investors who seek exposure to a high-growth market of investing and trading, yet at the current valuation, there appears to be little room for error.

Main Risks And Concerns

There are risks and concerns tied to Webull that could significantly impact the stock price performance in the short term.

The financial brokerage firm currently trades at a substantial premium versus both its peers and the average company in the S&P500, which leaves little room for error. If the company's top-line growth weakens or may be clouded by uncertainties, sentiment could dramatically shift, which could result in continued downward momentum for its stock.

The investing and trading landscape has fierce competition. Although this appears to be a high-growth market, there are several well-known players competing for customers. Such all-in-one companies as Robinhood, or more crypto-focused as Coinbase, create a challenging environment for the company to operate in. If Webull loses market share to competition, this could significantly impact sentiment and stress the stock price further.

Webull is heavily dependent on trading volume. Given its commission-free policy, the financial services provider heavily depends on retail investor sentiment and trading activity. If enthusiasm fades, the company may experience weaker-than-anticipated revenue growth. At this point, it would be challenging to justify the company trading at such a premium.

On the other hand, if none of the above materializes, the stock price could continue trading at such levels. If management can successfully expand into other markets, and that translates to both top and bottom-line growth, the company may rebound from here. Until then, investors should remain cautious and monitor sentiment around this company.

Conclusion: Premium Is High, And Valuation Demands Patience — A Hold

I believe that Webull may appear to be a compelling opportunity for investors who seek exposure in a high-growth market. Investors may have seen the recent success of Robinhood and Coinbase, highlighting massive returns for their investors, but it does not necessarily mean that Webull can mirror their success.

The company trades at a rich valuation, which demands patience. Based on my analysis, although management can deliver a double-digit revenue growth further, it may not lead to stock price appreciation. Premium is high, and investors may seek cheaper alternatives in the market. On the upside, BULL's global expansion appears promising, and that could act as a major tailwind in the years ahead.

Nevertheless, I rate Webull Corporation a Hold with a $12 price target over the next 12 months. This presents 10% upside possibility and market-like returns. I don't think it could significantly outperform the benchmark without a significant top and bottom line growth catalyst. 2 Wall Street analysts forecast a $18.50 price target over the next 12 months, indicating about 70% upside possibility. I don't feel that bullish, but if the company surprises, anything can happen.

data.title: Hims & Hers: Use This Drop Before It's Too Late
data.description:
Hims & Hers (NYSE:HIMS) has experienced a hefty sell-off of ~22% from its highs in recent weeks.

In my last article on the company in May, I rated the company a Buy, highlighting a potential undervaluation when underlying the growth prospects of the telehealth market.

In this article we are going to analyze the recent drop, explore potential risks, revamp the valuation, and prepare a checklist for the next earnings on 11/3/2025.

What Caused The Recent Sell-Off?

Volatility is normal for Hims & Hers. As can be seen nicely in the chart above. In my opinion, the most recent drop can be attributed to three things:

1. Insider Selling

Over the last year, insiders have been constantly selling the stock. Understandably, this is leading to lower investor confidence.

I personally, however, think that the importance of this is highly exaggerated. The CEO, Andrew Dudum, alone is currently still holding ~4.3% of the company.

2. Growth Slowdown

In the last earnings, Hims & Hers declared phenomenal YoY growth of 89% in subscribers and 73% in revenue. While this sounds great, HIMS didn't manage to grow subscribers or revenue QoQ.

According to management, this decline was mainly caused by the off-boarding of some GLP-1 subscribers. And at least on the subscriber front, most of this was offset by robust growth in dermatology, oral weight loss, and daily sexual health offerings.

3. Negative Cash Flow

In Q2, HIMS also declared negative cash flow. This was driven by working capital investments.

The balance sheet, however, remains robust with more than $1.1 billion in cash and short-term investments, caused by a recent convertible debt offering.

Management expects the Free Cash Flow to return to positive levels in the second half of 2025.

Risks To Consider

Continuous insider selling can have a permanent negative impact on investor confidence.

The company also needs their recent investments in marketing, growth, and talent to lead to higher growth once again. Should incremental ROI decline, margins might also erode in the long run. Therefore, the company needs to use the next quarter to prove to its investors that the latest quarter was just a small hiccup and they are able to sustainably get back to growing subscribers, revenue, and average revenue per user.

Although management claims that they expect no significant tariff pressure, this could still create problems, especially when it comes to API (Active Pharmaceutical Ingredient) sourcing and their planned international expansion.

Their increasing focus on international expansion in itself also poses risks. Expanding overseas might cause concentration problems and add complexity to operations and regulations. Maintaining robust U.S. growth is, on the other hand, also essential.

Despite diversifying, the business is still vulnerable to the crowded and competitive GLP-1 market (as can be seen in the revenue drop in Q2) as well as other new treatments; execution risk, therefore, in general is high.

Valuation

To evaluate the company, we first need some general assumptions:

2025 Revenue and Profitability: For 2025, I assumed that HIMS manages to achieve the average of management's FY 2025 guidance. Resulting in revenues of $2.35 billion and adjusted EBITDA of $310 million.

Profitability: The company expects to achieve an adjusted EBITDA of $1.3 billion and revenues of $6.5 billion in 2030, resulting in a margin of 20%.

Considering the current margin of 13% and the target rate for 2030, we can therefore expect the following development:

Financial year 2025(e) 2026(e) 2027(e) 2028(e) 2029(e) 2030(e) 2031(e) 2032(e)
Adj. EBITDA Margin 13.0% 14.5% 16.0% 17.5% 19.0% 20.0% 20.0% 20.0%

Free Cash Flow Conversion: Cash conversion has been at 112% and 118% of adj. EBITDA in 2023 and 2024, respectively. For some additional margin of safety, I assumed a rate of 100% for our analysis.

WACC and Perpetuity Growth: For the WACC, I assumed 10%, which is way below the current rate of ~8%. 3.5% as perpetuity growth also seems fitting considering the very high historical growth rates.

Required Growth

With these assumptions, Hims & Hers needs to grow by ~13% p.a. to justify their current valuation.

Realistic Growth

Like mentioned above, management is expecting to generate revenues of $6.5 billion in 2030. From current levels, this can be translated to an average top line growth rate of 22.6% per year until 2030.

This is also pretty much in line with the expected market growth rates of the telehealth market as a whole, further solidifying management's guidance.

  • Grand View Research: 24.6%
  • Market.us: 22.8%
  • Towardshealthcare.com: 24.7%

By assuming this growth, Hims & Hers could currently be undervalued by up to 61%, according to our DCF.

Checklist For The Next Earnings

Out of the risks and the potential reasons for the sell-off mentioned, I want to see the following things in the Q3 earnings on the 3rd of November:

  • QoQ growth
  • Positive free cash flow
  • Continued high profitability or even improvement
  • No/less insider selling

Conclusion

Considering the growth potential as well as the financials and historical growth, the recent drop seems like a compelling buy opportunity.

With the current valuation, Hims & Hers needs to grow their revenue by approx. 13% per year for the next eight years to justify their current valuation. Should the company manage to grow at the underlying market rate or management's 2030 guidance come to fruition, HIMS is currently undervalued by up to 61%.

I expect the company to improve their free cash flow situation as well as achieve solid quarter-over-quarter growth in Q3 of 2025. This could lead to a price jump, as it would negate most of the reasons for the current drop. That's why I currently rate the company a Strong Buy, and investors who want to participate in the telehealth market should use the stock's current weakness before it eventually gains traction again.

data.title: Dry-Powder Options To Replace XLFT.PR.A Redemption
data.description:
The XAI Octagon Floating Rate & Alternative Income Trust (NYSE:XFLT) announced it will redeem its Series A preferred (XFLT.PR.A). There are only a few months left until maturity, so it's not a huge surprise. The stock has been a great dry-powder asset while delivering a yield of around 7.5% since it was added about 6 months ago to our Income Portfolios. With it going away, we are on the lookout for other dry-powder securities with yields north of 7%.

Since its inception, XFLT.PR.A has been admirably stable amid all the volatility that we have seen over the last few years. This ability to keep its value across the cycle is an important feature of dry-powder assets, and it allows investors to take advantage of developing opportunities elsewhere, such as the current sell-off in the BDC sector.

Recall that XFLT as well as EIC are the lower-octane CLO CEFs with sizable non-CLO Equity exposure, the former being allocated primarily to loans and the latter primarily to CLO Debt. This makes the preferreds of these funds significantly lower risk than the preferreds from funds like OXLC, ECC, OCCI, CCIF and others in the sector. At the same time, the market does not often differentiate among these funds, with preferreds yields typically trading at similar levels.

Before looking at alternatives, let's review why CLO CEF senior securities remain very attractive and why they are a key part of our Income Portfolios.

Why CLO Senior Securities Are Worth A Look

The CLO senior security sector is a niche area of the broader income sector. It includes preferreds and baby bonds issued by investment funds (both CEFs and BDCs) that primarily allocate to CLO Equity and Debt securities.

What makes the sector attractive is not just the risk/reward on offer but also a number of additional factors. Specifically, because CEF and BDC sectors are relatively bifurcated (few income investors [and analysts] are well-versed in both sectors), there are greater relative value opportunities than in other parts of the income market. And because the key risk metric investors use (i.e. asset coverage) is partial and misleading, it's easy for investors to go astray. Both of these factors generate a significant amount of alpha for investors willing to put in the work.

The preferreds and baby bonds of the various CLO Equity funds tend to be overshadowed by their common shares, which deliver incredible yields at an even more incredible volatility. However, for investors who want to acquire income securities with historic price stability and attractive absolute and risk-adjusted yields, senior securities may be more appealing.

Finally, unlike their common share counterparts, baby bonds and term preferreds deliver on their yield in terms of performance (barring a default, naturally). We cannot say the same of the common shares of CLO Equity funds which have tended to deliver performance (measured as the total NAV return) well below their yields.

The performance of CLO Equity common shares has been particularly disappointing this year as the sector has been under pressure from an expected easing cycle by the Fed as well as a wave of loan refinancing, which has pushed spreads tighter. Year-to-date, CLO Equity CEFs are sitting on NAV losses of around 20%.

Over the longer-term what we see is that the common shares of the legacy CLO Equity funds have underperformed their preferred shares. Although the latest leg of underperformance was due largely to discount widening of the funds, driven by distribution cuts, what the charts show is that even on a trend basis, the common shares struggle to overcome their much lower-yielding and boring preferreds.

This is what the performance chart looks like for ECC common and preferred shares.

This is what it looks like for OXLC.

And this is what it looks like for OCCI.

This dynamic of more risk / less reward is not unheard of, but it certainly does run counter to the two generic market mantras that, over the longer term 1) higher-yielding securities will tend to outperform lower-yielding securities and 2) higher-risk securities will tend to outperform lower-risk securities.

For our part, we have recognized these dynamics for some time and have largely avoided the common shares of these funds in our Income Portfolios. It should be said that we don't take the more extreme view that the common shares of these funds are uninvestable, but rather, they are best left to more tactical opportunities.

The CLO Equity CEF senior security subsector has a number of unusual and attractive features. First, nearly all securities have mandatory maturities, even the preferreds (save for 2 exceptions). This significantly lowers their duration, derisks them, and makes them less negatively convex from large changes in interest rates.

Secondly, a number of securities in the sector are monthly payers, which is both attractive for many investors and understates their yield relative to the usual quarterly-paying preferreds due to the additional compounding.

Thirdly, the sector features unusual mandatory redemption features that require the funds to redeem the securities if their asset coverage remains below a given level.

Fourthly, the sector has enjoyed repeated support from fund managers who tend to repurchase shares of their preferreds and baby bonds during drawdowns (otherwise they would not be allowed to pay distributions on common shares). This not only improves the asset coverage of the senior securities but also supports their prices in the market.

Fifthly, being funds, these securities are more predictable in their performance than those of "typical" companies, which might be derailed by fraud, loss of a key customer, supplier issues and other factors typical of the corporate world. Fund companies can certainly struggle, but they will struggle for systemic reasons with few if any idiosyncratic surprises so prevalent in the traditional corporate sector.

Alternatives to XFLT.PR.A

What are the drier-powder alternatives to XFLT.PR.A? To replace the stock, we are happy to remain in the same sector. We are not looking for cash assets, as we tend to view the opportunity cost of giving up an additional 3-4% of spread as too high.

BDC OXSQ bonds remain attractive as the company's portfolio is primarily in first lien loans, with CLO Equity making up 37%. There is also no secured facility ahead of the bonds. The company's 2030 bond OXSQH is trading at a yield of 7.66%.

The CEF EIC is another minority CLO Equity fund, being allocated primarily to CLO Debt. It is funded entirely with preferreds, meaning there are no other credit holders ahead of the preferred shareholders. The 2029 preferred EICC trades at a yield of 7.6%.

Finally, the CLO Equity CEF ECC bonds are senior in the capital structure (the fund also has 3 preferreds), meaning the preferreds will soak up any losses before the bondholders are hit. The 2031 bond ECCW trades at a yield of 8.1%.

CLO fund senior securities remain compelling decent-yield / drier-powder assets in the current market environment.
